section.title: The Counsel of Ahithophel and Hushai
section.sectionRationale: The rebellion's outcome hangs on a contest of counsel. Ahithophel gives Absalom shrewd, deadly advice to strike David at once; Hushai, David's secret agent, gives flattering, delaying counsel instead, and Absalom takes it, because the LORD had purposed to thwart Ahithophel's good counsel and bring disaster on Absalom. Warned in time, David crosses the Jordan to safety; the humiliated Ahithophel hangs himself. The movement is the quiet turning point where God overrules the wisest human strategy to save His king.
section.thesis: The fate of the kingdom turns not on the cleverest human counsel but on the hidden purpose of God, who ordains that Absalom reject Ahithophel's sound advice and follow Hushai's, so that David escapes, the traitor Ahithophel perishes, and the reader sees that no wisdom or scheme can stand against the LORD's determined purpose to save His anointed.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Ahithophel's deadly counsel and Hushai's counter-counsel, verses 1 to 13; the LORD thwarts Ahithophel to bring disaster on Absalom, verse 14; the warning that saves David and his crossing of the Jordan, verses 15 to 22; and Ahithophel's suicide with David's provision at Mahanaim, verses 23 to 29.

verses.text: Furthermore, Ahithophel said to Absalom, “Let me choose twelve thousand men and set out tonight in pursuit of David.
I will attack him while he is weak and weary; I will throw him into a panic, and all the people with him will flee; I will strike down only the king
and bring all the people back to you as a bride returning to her husband. You seek the life of only one man; then all the people will be at peace.”
This proposal seemed good to Absalom and all the elders of Israel.
Then Absalom said, “Summon Hushai the Archite as well, and let us hear what he too has to say.”
So Hushai came to Absalom, who told him, “Ahithophel has spoken this proposal. Should we carry it out? If not, what do you say?”
Hushai replied, “This time the {{etsah|advice}} of Ahithophel is not sound.”

He continued, “You know your father and his men. They are mighty men, and as fierce as a wild bear robbed of her cubs. Moreover, your father is a man of war who will not spend the night with the troops.
Surely by now he is hiding in a cave or some other location. If some of your troops fall first, whoever hears of it will say, ‘There has been a slaughter among the troops who follow Absalom.’
Then even the most valiant soldier with the heart of a lion will melt with fear, because all Israel knows that your father is a mighty man who has valiant men with him.
Instead, I advise that all Israel from Dan to Beersheba, a multitude like the sand on the seashore, be gathered to you, and that you yourself lead them into battle.
Then we will attack David wherever we find him, and we will descend on him like dew on the ground. And of all the men with him, not even one will remain.
If he retreats to a city, all Israel will bring ropes to that city, and we will drag it down to the valley until not even a pebble can be found there.”

Then Absalom and all the men of Israel said, “The advice of Hushai the Archite is better than that of Ahithophel.” For the LORD had purposed to {{parar|thwart}} the good counsel of Ahithophel in order to bring disaster on Absalom.

units.label: The warning that saves David
units.note: cross over the river at once

verses.text: So Hushai told Zadok and Abiathar, the priests, “This is what Ahithophel has advised Absalom and the elders of Israel, and this is what I have advised.
Now send quickly and tell David, ‘Do not spend the night at the fords of the wilderness, but be sure to cross over. Otherwise the king and all the people with him will be swallowed up.’”

Now Jonathan and Ahimaaz were staying at En-rogel, where a servant girl would come and pass along information to them. They in turn would go and inform King David, for they dared not be seen entering the city.
But a young man did see them and told Absalom. So the two left quickly and came to the house of a man in Bahurim. He had a well in his courtyard, and they climbed down into it.
Then the man’s wife took a covering, spread it over the mouth of the well, and scattered grain over it so nobody would know a thing.

When Absalom’s servants came to the woman at the house, they asked, “Where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?” “They have crossed over the brook,” she replied. The men searched but did not find them, so they returned to Jerusalem.

After the men had gone, Ahimaaz and Jonathan climbed up out of the well and went to inform King David, saying, “Get up and cross over the river at once, for Ahithophel has given this advice against you.”
So David and all the people with him got up and crossed the Jordan. By daybreak, there was no one left who had not crossed the Jordan.

units.label: Ahithophel's end, David provided for
units.note: the traitor perishes, the king is fed

verses.text: When Ahithophel saw that his advice had not been followed, he saddled his donkey and set out for his house in his hometown. He put his affairs in order and hanged himself. So he died and was buried in his father’s tomb.
Then David went to Mahanaim, and Absalom crossed the Jordan with all the men of Israel.

Absalom had appointed Amasa over the army in place of Joab. Amasa was the son of a man named Ithra, the Ishmaelite who had married Abigail, the daughter of Nahash and sister of Zeruiah the mother of Joab.
So the Israelites and Absalom camped in the land of Gilead.
When David came to Mahanaim, he was met by Shobi son of Nahash from Rabbah of the Ammonites, Machir son of Ammiel from Lo-debar, and Barzillai the Gileadite from Rogelim.
They brought beds, basins, and earthen vessels, as well as wheat, barley, flour, roasted grain, beans, lentils,
honey, curds, sheep, and cheese from the herd for David and his people to eat. For they said, “The people have become hungry, exhausted, and thirsty in the wilderness.”

etsah.gloss: Counsel, advice, plan, purpose. The whole chapter is a contest of etsah: Ahithophel's counsel, esteemed almost as the word of God, against Hushai's. The word also describes God's own purpose, and the drama turns on the truth that human counsel, however wise, cannot stand against the counsel of the LORD, Proverbs 19:21.

parar.gloss: To break, frustrate, annul, bring to nothing. The narrator states plainly that the LORD had purposed to parar, thwart, the good counsel of Ahithophel to bring disaster on Absalom. The word reveals the hidden hand of God overruling the best human strategy, breaking the plan that would have destroyed His anointed, so that God's saving purpose prevails.

basic.context: The rebellion now hinges on a single decision. Ahithophel, whose counsel was esteemed almost like the word of God, urges Absalom to let him take twelve thousand men and strike the exhausted David that very night, killing only the king and bringing the people back peacefully. It is brilliant, ruthless, and would almost certainly have worked. But Absalom, perhaps out of vanity or caution, wants a second opinion and summons Hushai, David's secret agent. Hushai, knowing the danger, argues against immediate attack. He flatters Absalom's fears, magnifies David's prowess as a warrior, and counsels instead that all Israel be gathered into one overwhelming host led by Absalom himself, a plan that sounds grander but fatally gives David time to escape. Absalom and the elders prefer Hushai's advice. The narrator then lifts the veil: this happened because the LORD had purposed to thwart Ahithophel's good counsel in order to bring disaster on Absalom. Hushai sends warning to David through a relay of messengers who narrowly escape capture by hiding in a well, and David crosses the Jordan to safety by daybreak. Ahithophel, seeing his counsel rejected and the cause doomed, goes home, sets his affairs in order, and hangs himself. David reaches Mahanaim, where loyal friends bring abundant provisions. The chapter raises the question of who really governs the outcome of events, human wisdom or the sovereign purpose of God.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative with an explicit theological key in verse 14: God was behind the outcome. The chapter does not deny the reality of human deliberation, Ahithophel really was wiser and Hushai really did deceive, but it insists that God sovereignly directed the decision to save David. Hushai's deception operates within the messy realities of a rebellion; the text reports it as the means God used, without turning it into a general endorsement of lying. Ahithophel's suicide is reported soberly as the end of a brilliant man whose betrayal came to nothing. The chapter teaches God's providence over human affairs, not a formula for political intrigue.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is a contest of etsah, counsel: Ahithophel's counsel, prized almost as the word of God, against Hushai's. Yet the decisive word is that the LORD had purposed to parar, to thwart, break, and bring to nothing, the good counsel of Ahithophel. The two words together carry the chapter's message: the wisest human etsah cannot stand once God determines to parar it, for the counsel of the LORD alone prevails.
basic.typology: God's hidden overruling to rescue His anointed king from a plot that should have succeeded foreshadows His greater work of raising up and vindicating His true Anointed. And Ahithophel, the trusted intimate who betrayed David, shared his table, and then went out and hanged himself, is a striking type of Judas, who betrayed the Son of David, and likewise hanged himself, so that even the traitor's end points to the passion of Christ.
basic.christ: Two threads point to Christ. First, God's sovereign thwarting of Ahithophel's counsel to save David displays the same providence by which God worked through and over human schemes to accomplish salvation in Christ, whom no plot could finally defeat. Second, Ahithophel is a clear type of Judas: both were trusted friends who ate at the table of the Lord's anointed, both betrayed him, and both hanged themselves. Where Ahithophel's betrayal of David came to nothing against God's purpose, Judas's betrayal of Jesus was itself woven into God's saving plan at the cross.
basic.god: God is the true governor of the chapter's outcome, the one who purposed to thwart the wisest counsel and so save His king, revealing that His purpose stands over all human wisdom and scheming. This is the God of Proverbs, that many are the plans in a person's heart but the counsel of the LORD prevails. He works providentially through ordinary means, Absalom's vanity, Hushai's words, a servant girl, a well, a covering of grain, to accomplish His determined ends, showing His sovereignty is exercised in and through the fabric of human events.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 21 declares that no wisdom or counsel can prevail against the LORD. Proverbs 19 says the LORD's purpose stands over many human plans. Matthew 27 and Acts 1 record Judas's betrayal and suicide, echoing Ahithophel. And Psalm 41, quoted of Judas in John 13, laments a close friend who ate David's bread and turned against him.
basic.oneStory: The kingdom's fate turns not on the cleverest strategy but on the hidden purpose of God, who thwarts the wisest counsel to save His anointed and lets the traitor perish by his own hand. This sovereign rescue, and the traitor's end so like Judas's, point forward to God's saving work in Christ, the true Anointed whom no betrayal or scheme could defeat, and whom God vindicated according to His unbreakable purpose.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as a debate with a divine verdict at its center: two speeches of counsel, the people's choice, and the narrator's revelation in verse 14 that God was behind it. The suspenseful escape of the messengers, with the well and the grain, echoes Rahab hiding the spies at Jericho. Ahithophel's methodical suicide and David's abundant provision at Mahanaim close the chapter with the fates of traitor and true king starkly contrasted.
context.historical: A swift night strike on an exhausted, unorganized enemy was militarily sound, which is why Ahithophel's counsel was so dangerous. Hushai exploited Absalom's inexperience and desire for a glorious, decisive victory led by himself. En-rogel was a spring near Jerusalem; hiding in a courtyard well covered with grain was a plausible concealment. Ahithophel's putting his affairs in order before hanging himself reflects deliberate, dignified despair. Mahanaim, east of the Jordan, was a defensible base, and Barzillai and others there had reason to support David.
context.canonical: The chapter is a prime biblical illustration of divine providence over human affairs, often cited alongside Proverbs on the supremacy of God's counsel. The Ahithophel-Judas parallel is drawn by the church from the Gospels' use of Psalm 41 and the shared detail of suicide by hanging. The rescue of the rejected king by God's overruling of a superior plan resonates with the whole Bible's theme that God's purposes to save cannot be thwarted.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the outcome of the rebellion was determined not by the superior human counsel of Ahithophel but by the sovereign purpose of God, who thwarted that counsel to save David, so that the reader learns to see God's hidden hand governing even the deliberations and schemes of people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a war council, a deception, and a suicide within a specific crisis; it does not prescribe deceit as a strategy or comment approvingly on every human act. What endures is the truth that God's purpose overrules the wisest human counsel, that no scheme can finally defeat God's plan to save His anointed, and that betrayal of the Lord's anointed ends in ruin.

debates.question: How does verse 14 relate God's sovereignty to the real human choices in the chapter?

views.label: Genuine deliberation truly governed by God's purpose
views.case: The men really weighed the two counsels and chose Hushai's, and Ahithophel's advice really was better, yet the narrator says the LORD had purposed to thwart it; Scripture consistently presents God's sovereignty working through, not around, real human decisions, so that Absalom freely chose the worse counsel and yet did exactly what God had ordained, illustrating that the counsel of the LORD stands over the plans of many hearts, Proverbs 19:21.

views.label: A providential answer to David's prayer
views.case: David had prayed in 15:31 that the LORD would turn Ahithophel's counsel into foolishness, and this chapter is the answer; on this reading verse 14 highlights God responding to His servant's plea by so ordering events, through Hushai and Absalom's choice, that the deadly counsel was set aside, showing God's providence as personal and responsive as well as sovereign.

debates.bearing: Both agree that God's sovereign purpose governed the outcome without erasing the reality of human deliberation; the chapter teaches that God rules over the counsels of people to accomplish His saving will, answering prayer and overruling wisdom alike, a comfort that His purposes cannot be defeated by the cleverest opposition.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter stages a contest of etsah, counsel: the advice of Ahithophel, esteemed in those days almost as if one had inquired of the word of God, set against the advice of Hushai. Ahithophel's counsel is plainly the sounder, a swift strike that would have destroyed David that very night. Yet the narrator lifts the veil with one decisive statement: the LORD had purposed to parar, to break, frustrate, and bring to nothing, the good counsel of Ahithophel, in order to bring disaster on Absalom, verse 14. The verb parar reveals the hidden hand of God shattering the best human strategy. Together the two words deliver the chapter's message and answer David's earlier prayer that Ahithophel's counsel be turned to foolishness: the wisest etsah of the shrewdest counselor cannot stand for a moment once the LORD determines to parar it, for as Proverbs teaches, many are the plans in a person's heart, but the counsel of the LORD alone prevails.

typology.type: God overruling a superior plot to rescue His anointed king
typology.fulfillment: God's sovereign work through and over human schemes to accomplish salvation in Christ, whom no plot could defeat, Acts 2:23-24.

typology.type: Ahithophel, the trusted friend who betrayed David and hanged himself
typology.fulfillment: Judas, who betrayed the Son of David and likewise hanged himself, Matthew 26:14-16 and 27:5.

typology.type: The intimate who ate at the king's table and turned traitor
typology.fulfillment: Christ's betrayer, of whom Psalm 41:9 is quoted, John 13:18.

study.christ: Two threads bind this chapter to Christ. First, God's sovereign thwarting of Ahithophel's superior counsel to save David displays the same providence by which God worked through and over human schemes to accomplish salvation in His true Anointed; just as no plot could finally defeat David, so the plot against Jesus, though carried out by wicked hands, was itself within God's set purpose and ended in resurrection, Acts 2:23-24. Second, Ahithophel is one of Scripture's clearest types of Judas: both were trusted, intimate counselors who had eaten at the table of the Lord's anointed, Psalm 41:9 and John 13:18, both betrayed him, and both went out and hanged themselves, Matthew 27:5. Yet where Ahithophel's betrayal of David came to nothing against God's purpose, Judas's betrayal of Jesus was woven by God into the very means of salvation, so that the traitor's dark deed served the redemption of the world.
study.god: God is the true governor of this chapter's outcome, the one who purposed to thwart the wisest counsel and so rescue His king, revealing that His purpose stands sovereign over all human wisdom, deliberation, and scheming. This is the God of Proverbs, before whom no wisdom or counsel can prevail, and whose purpose stands over the many plans of the human heart. He works providentially through the most ordinary means, Absalom's vanity, Hushai's persuasive words, a servant girl's message, two young men, a courtyard well, a woman's covering of grain, to accomplish His determined ends, so that His sovereignty is exercised not apart from but within the fabric of human events. He is also the God who answers prayer, for David had asked that Ahithophel's counsel be turned to folly, and here it is.

crossReferences.note: David's prayer that the LORD turn Ahithophel's counsel into foolishness, answered in this chapter's overruling of that counsel.
There is no wisdom, no understanding, no counsel that can prevail against the LORD, the truth enacted in verse 14.
The lament of a close friend who shared the king's bread and lifted his heel against him, applied to Ahithophel and, by Jesus, to Judas in John 13:18.
Judas, having betrayed innocent blood, went out and hanged himself, echoing Ahithophel's end.

study.oneStory: The kingdom's fate turns not on the cleverest strategy but on the hidden purpose of God, who thwarts the wisest human counsel to save His anointed king and lets the brilliant traitor perish by his own hand. This sovereign rescue, and the traitor's end so strikingly like Judas's, point forward to God's saving work in Christ, the true Anointed whom no betrayal or scheme could defeat, and whom God vindicated according to His unbreakable purpose. The chapter assures every reader that the counsel of the LORD stands, and that His plan to save His people cannot be overturned by the most formidable opposition.

quiz.q: Whose counsel was actually the wiser and more dangerous to David?

options.text: Ahithophel's, to strike the exhausted David at once that very night, which would likely have succeeded.
options.note: Verses 1-3 give Ahithophel's shrewd, deadly plan that the text calls 'good counsel.'

options.text: Hushai's, to gather all Israel into one great army led by Absalom.
options.note: Hushai's counsel was the delaying, weaker plan designed to save David, not the wiser one.

options.text: Ahithophel's, whose swift strike posed the real threat to David.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking detail that Ahithophel's counsel was esteemed almost like the word of God.

options.text: Both counsels were equally sound and it hardly mattered which was chosen.
options.note: The text says Ahithophel's was the good counsel that God thwarted; the choice mattered decisively.

quiz.q: Why did Absalom follow Hushai's inferior advice instead of Ahithophel's?

options.text: Because the LORD had purposed to thwart Ahithophel's good counsel in order to bring disaster on Absalom.
options.note: Verse 14 gives the theological key: God overruled the outcome.

options.text: Because Hushai's plan was obviously the more sensible of the two.
options.note: Hushai's plan was actually the weaker; the text says God thwarted the good counsel of Ahithophel.

options.text: Because God was directing the decision to save David.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific aim stated in the text, to bring disaster on Absalom.

options.text: Because Absalom distrusted Ahithophel personally.
options.note: The text attributes the choice to the LORD's purpose, not to personal distrust of Ahithophel.

quiz.q: What happens to Ahithophel after his counsel is rejected?

options.text: He goes home, puts his affairs in order, and hangs himself.
options.note: Verse 23 records Ahithophel's deliberate suicide once his advice is set aside.

options.text: He switches sides and rejoins David's camp.
options.note: He does not rejoin David; he takes his own life.

options.text: He takes his own life in despair over the doomed rebellion.
options.note: True, but this option omits the sober detail that he first set his house in order, a deliberate act.

options.text: He is executed by Absalom for giving bad advice.
options.note: Ahithophel is not executed; he hangs himself after his counsel is rejected.

quiz.q: How does the chapter portray God's relationship to the human decisions in it?

options.text: God sovereignly governs the outcome through real human deliberation, overruling the wisest counsel to save His king and answer David's prayer.
options.note: Verse 14, with 15:31, shows God's purpose working through genuine choices, Proverbs 19:21.

options.text: God stands back and lets the best human strategy simply prevail.
options.note: The opposite; God thwarts the best strategy to accomplish His purpose.

options.text: God works His purpose through the choices people freely make.
options.note: True, but this option omits that God overruled the wiser counsel specifically to save David and doom Absalom.

options.text: God changes the facts so that Hushai's plan was really better.
options.note: The text calls Ahithophel's the good counsel; God did not make Hushai's wiser but ordained that it be followed.

quiz.q: How does Ahithophel foreshadow a figure in the Gospels?

options.text: He is a type of Judas: a trusted intimate who ate at the king's table, betrayed the Lord's anointed, and hanged himself.
options.note: The parallels with Judas are drawn in Psalm 41:9, John 13:18, and Matthew 27:5.

options.text: He foreshadows Peter, who denied Jesus but was restored.
options.note: Ahithophel betrays and destroys himself; he parallels Judas, not the restored Peter.

options.text: He prefigures the betrayer of the Son of David who came to a ruinous end.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific, striking shared detail of hanging himself and eating at the king's table.

options.text: He represents the faithful counselor who serves the king to the end.
options.note: The faithful counselor here is Hushai; Ahithophel is the traitor whose end mirrors Judas.

questions: The outcome hung on God thwarting the wisest human plan. Where are you tempted to think that the cleverest strategy, rather than God's purpose, will finally decide your situation?

David had prayed that Ahithophel's counsel be turned to folly, and this chapter is the answer. How does seeing God answer that prayer through ordinary events encourage you to keep praying about outcomes you cannot control?
Ahithophel was brilliant, respected, and utterly wrong to set himself against God's anointed. How does this warn against trusting your own wisdom when it runs contrary to God's revealed will?
God worked through a servant girl, a well, and a covering of grain to save His king. Where might God be quietly working through small, ordinary things in your life right now?
Ahithophel's betrayal of the king came to nothing against God's purpose. How does the certainty that no scheme can finally defeat God's plan to save give you confidence in Christ?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that the reading 'You seek the life of only one man' follows the Septuagint and Dead Sea Scrolls; the Masoretic Text reads differently but conveys the same point, that only David's death was sought.

The narrator's statement that 'the LORD had purposed to thwart the good counsel of Ahithophel' is the theological key of the chapter, showing God's sovereign providence overruling superior human counsel to save David, and answering his prayer in 15:31.
Ahithophel's suicide by hanging, after his counsel was rejected, is one of only a few such deaths in Scripture and is echoed by Judas in Matthew 27:5, part of the Ahithophel-Judas typology.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 17:1-29. Em dashes in the source at verse 11 were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file.
